source_paper_title: A Theory of Alternating Paths and Blossoms from the Perspective of Minimum Length
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Vazirani_2024, title={A Theory of Alternating Paths and Blossoms from the Perspective of Minimum Length}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2020.0388}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2020.0388}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Vazirani, Vijay V.}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={2009–2047} }

Section 9.3, Remark 9 (page 2044), and Section 10, final open question (page 2046).

Remark 9. A question arising from Theorem 8 is whether there is a linear time implementation of \(\mathrm{bud}^*\) in the pointer model. Micali and Vazirani [24] had claimed, without proof, that path compression by itself suffices to achieve this. They stated that because of the special structure of blossoms, a charging argument could be given that assigns a constant cost to each edge. This claim is left as a problem.

Finally, here is an open question. Is there a linear time implementation of a phase of MV on the pointer model? ... Is it the case that the MV algorithm, implemented using the set union algorithm of Tarjan [31], actually runs in linear time per phase? ... Using these facts and structural properties established in this paper, can one show that the total time devoted to set union in a phase is bounded by \(O(m)\)?
title: Linear-time Micali–Vazirani phase implementation on pointer machines via path compression
problem_definition_latex:
On the pointer machine model, analyze the total time per phase spent on maintaining and querying \(\mathrm{bud}^*(\cdot)\) (the iterated-bud representative) in the MV algorithm.

1) Prove or disprove that implementing \(\mathrm{bud}^*\) using path compression (as suggested for MV) yields total time \(O(m)\) per phase.

Equivalently (as an alternative formulation also raised in the paper):

2) Prove or disprove that when MV is implemented with Tarjan's disjoint-set union algorithm to support \(\mathrm{bud}^*(\cdot)\), the total time devoted to disjoint-set operations within one phase is \(O(m)\) (so the phase runs in linear time on the pointer model).

The analysis should use only the sequence/structure of disjoint-set \(\mathrm{find}\)/\(\mathrm{union}\) operations generated by an MV phase (including DDFS calls and petal/bud formation) and not assume worst-case union--find bounds a priori.
latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be an undirected graph with \(|V|=n\), \(|E|=m\), and let \(M\subseteq E\) be a matching. An \(M\)-alternating path is a simple path whose edges alternate between \(M\) and \(E\setminus M\). An \(M\)-augmenting path is an alternating path whose endpoints are unmatched vertices (with respect to \(M\)); augmenting along such a path increases \(|M|\) by 1.

The Micali--Vazirani (MV) algorithm finds a maximum-cardinality matching by repeating phases. In one phase it finds a maximal set of vertex-disjoint minimum-length augmenting paths (with respect to the current matching \(M\)) and augments along all of them. A phase uses a graph-search subroutine called double depth-first search (DDFS) to discover either an augmenting path or a certain contracted structure (petal/blossom). During a phase, the algorithm maintains a dynamically changing parent-like map \(\mathrm{bud}:V\to V\) that maps each vertex to the current \"bud\" representing the petal containing it, and the iterated map \(\mathrm{bud}^*(v)\) defined by repeated application of \(\mathrm{bud}\) until reaching a fixed point:
\[
\mathrm{bud}^*(v)=\begin{cases}
 v, & \text{if }\mathrm{bud}(v)=v,\\
 \mathrm{bud}^*(\mathrm{bud}(v)), & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
Computing \(\mathrm{bud}^*\) is implemented via a disjoint-set union (union--find) data structure, where unions correspond to newly formed petals/blossoms and finds return the current representative \(\mathrm{bud}^*(v)\). On a pointer machine model, standard union--find with path compression runs in \(O(\alpha(m,n))\) amortized time per operation, yielding \(O(m\,\alpha(m,n))\) time per phase if there are \(O(m)\) such operations.

The paper proves correctness of MV and shows a per-phase time bound \(O(m)\) on the RAM model and \(O(m\,\alpha(m,n))\) on the pointer model when union--find is used to maintain \(\mathrm{bud}^*\). It states that Micali and Vazirani claimed (without proof) that, due to special blossom structure in MV, path compression alone suffices to make the total time spent in maintaining \(\mathrm{bud}^*\) linear per phase on the pointer model, i.e., eliminating the extra \(\alpha(m,n)\) factor. The paper also poses a related question: whether the union--find based MV implementation already runs in linear time per phase because the number/structure of operations is more restricted than in worst-case union--find analysis.

On the pointer machine model, analyze the total time per phase spent on maintaining and querying \(\mathrm{bud}^*(\cdot)\) (the iterated-bud representative) in the MV algorithm.

1) Prove or disprove that implementing \(\mathrm{bud}^*\) using path compression (as suggested for MV) yields total time \(O(m)\) per phase.

Equivalently (as an alternative formulation also raised in the paper):

2) Prove or disprove that when MV is implemented with Tarjan's disjoint-set union algorithm to support \(\mathrm{bud}^*(\cdot)\), the total time devoted to disjoint-set operations within one phase is \(O(m)\) (so the phase runs in linear time on the pointer model).

The analysis should use only the sequence/structure of disjoint-set \(\mathrm{find}\)/\(\mathrm{union}\) operations generated by an MV phase (including DDFS calls and petal/bud formation) and not assume worst-case union--find bounds a priori.
location_in_paper: Section 9.3, Remark 9 (page 2044), and Section 10, final open question (page 2046).
open_problem_quote:
Remark 9. A question arising from Theorem 8 is whether there is a linear time implementation of \(\mathrm{bud}^*\) in the pointer model. Micali and Vazirani [24] had claimed, without proof, that path compression by itself suffices to achieve this. They stated that because of the special structure of blossoms, a charging argument could be given that assigns a constant cost to each edge. This claim is left as a problem.

Finally, here is an open question. Is there a linear time implementation of a phase of MV on the pointer model? ... Is it the case that the MV algorithm, implemented using the set union algorithm of Tarjan [31], actually runs in linear time per phase? ... Using these facts and structural properties established in this paper, can one show that the total time devoted to set union in a phase is bounded by \(O(m)\)?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement concerns an amortized time bound for a highly structured but still unbounded sequence of union--find operations arising from MV; searching for a finite counterexample sequence that forces superlinear total time is nontrivial and may require very large constructed phases with intricate blossom nesting, making brute-force automated instance search unlikely to be decisive.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can code the MV phase and instrument the exact sequence of \(\mathrm{find}\)/\(\mathrm{union}\) operations, then use evolutionary search to generate graphs that maximize the ratio of union--find pointer traversals to \(m\) under the MV execution. This provides a computable fitness signal, but it is only a proxy: even large empirical ratios do not constitute a proof of superlinear worst-case behavior, and the true worst cases may be hard to reach by heuristic search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
